query: evidence of propofol causing green urine

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: If propofol infusion syndrome is suspected, the patient will present with metabolic acidosis with elevated lactic acid. Cardiac derangements may include hypotension, bradycardia, right bundle branch block, cardiac arrhythmias, and asystole. ECG may show toxicity with a Brugada-like pattern and a convex ST-elevation in the suitable precordial leads. Musculoskeletal injury can occur in the form of rhabdomyolysis and myopathy. Other signs may reflect renal and hepatic abnormalities, such as oliguria or hepatomegaly. Urine has also been noted to have a red or green appearance. [4] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol -- Adverse Effects -- Adverse Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: Transient local pain at the injection site is the most common adverse reaction. This may be decreased by administering IV lidocaine before propofol bolus. Hypotension Myoclonus Occasionally has been seen to cause EKG changes (QT interval prolongation). This is rarely clinically significant. Discolored urine (a green tint); this adverse event is exceedingly rare [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol Toxicity -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical evaluation of the patient is essential to look for signs of organ dysfunction, especially if the patient cannot vocalize any discomfort due to the propofol sedation — signs such as dark urine from rhabdomyolysis or hepatomegaly. Monitoring lab values regularly upon initiating and during propofol infusion will be essential to ascertain any early signs of PRIS. Results may reflect multi-organ system dysfunction. Serum lab values can reveal a high anion gap metabolic acidosis, lactic acidosis, and lipemic plasma. Additionally, there may be elevated serum creatine kinase, serum myoglobin, serum urea, serum potassium, and liver enzymes. Hypertriglyceridemia is not unexpected due to the lipemic nature of the medication, which can lead to pancreatitis. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Urinalysis -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Color
retrieved_docs.content: Associations: Amber: Bile pigments Brown/Black (Tea-colored): Bile pigments, cascara, chloroquine, fava beans, homogentisic acid (alkaptonuria), levodopa, melanin or oxidized melanogen, methemoglobin, methyldopa, metronidazole, myoglobin, nitrofurantoin, primaquine, rhubarb, riboflavin, senna Dark Yellow: Concentrated specimen (dehydration, exercise) Green/Blue: Amitriptyline, asparagus, biliverdin, cimetidine, clorets (breath mint), indicans, indigo carmine, indomethacin, methocarbamol, methylene blue, promethazine, propofol, pseudomonal UTI, triamterene Orange: Bile pigments, carrots, coumadin, nitrofurantoin, phenothiazines, phenazopyridine, rifampin, vitamin C Pink/Red: Beets, blackberries, chlorpromazine, food dyes, hematuria, hemoglobinuria, menstrual contamination, myoglobinuria, phenolphthalein, porphyrins, rifampin, rhubarb, senna, thioridazine, uric acid crystals. [7] [14] A urine sample that turns red on standing suggests the presence of porphobilinogen, which is increased in acute porphyrias.

retrieved_docs.content: Not routinely reported Normal: Appears upon agitation and dissipates readily on standing Associations: Proteinuria, bile pigments, retrograde ejaculation, medications (phenazopyridine, etc.), non-specific/unexplained [7] [11] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Phenol Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis of the manifestations of phenol toxicity is broad, given that phenol exerts effects on nearly all organ systems. Dermal exposure may present similar to other potent acid or alkali exposures, but the characteristic brown-stained skin may be a clue to phenol. Hypopigmented skin may also present with hydrofluoric acid dermal exposures. The combination of seizures and cardiovascular collapse is very non-specific and is observable with a broad range of life-threatening toxin exposures. The characteristic blue-green urine is also a possible feature with the therapeutic use of propofol, a phenol-derived pharmaceutical.

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Determine the evaluation of a patient with propofol toxicity. Evaluate the history and physical exam findings typically present in patients with propofol toxicity. Determine the treatment options for propofol toxicity. Communicate modalities to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members in order to improve outcomes for patients affected by propofol toxicity. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Propofol is an intravenous anesthetic used for procedural sedation, during monitored anesthesia care, or as an induction agent for general anesthesia. It may be administered as a bolus or an infusion, or some combination of the two. Propofol is prepared in a lipid emulsion which gives it the characteristic milky white appearance. Strict aseptic technique must be used when drawing up propofol as the emulsion can support microbial growth. It has both approved and off-label uses. This activity outlines the indications, mechanism of action, methods of administration, important adverse effects, contraindications, monitoring, and toxicity of propofol, so providers can direct patient anesthesia where it is indicated, as part of the interprofessional team.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1477
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a broad class of agents used to induce reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. Within this class, chloroform, enfluran (enflurane), fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran (isoflurane), methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol are recognized as specific child agents that share the overarching function of producing general anesthesia. Xenon is also classified as a type of general anesthetic, reflecting its role as an inhalational agent with anesthetic properties. Although these agents differ in chemical structure, potency, onset and recovery times, and side‑effect profiles, they are all integrated into the therapeutic repertoire for achieving controlled, reversible unconsciousness in patients undergoing operative or diagnostic interventions. Understanding the relationships among these individual anesthetic agents and the general anesthetic category supports informed selection, dosing, and monitoring strategies to optimize patient safety and perioperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1638
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse group of agents used to produce reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia for surgical procedures. The category “anesthetics, general” includes chloroform, enflurane, fencyclidine, flurothyl, isoflurane, methohexital, and propofol as child agents, each representing a distinct chemical class with specific pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic profiles. In addition, xenon—a noble gas—also falls under the classification of general anesthetics, indicating that it shares the core functional purpose of inducing general anesthesia despite its unique mechanism of action. Together, these agents illustrate the breadth of substances that can be employed to achieve controlled, reversible unconsciousness in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse class of pharmacologic agents used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. This class includes chloralosa, chloroform, enfluran, etomidát, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. Each of these agents functions as a “child” of the broader category of general anesthetics, reflecting their role as specific drugs that share the common purpose of producing and maintaining general anesthesia. While all serve the same overarching clinical goal, they differ in chemical structure, mechanism of action, potency, onset and duration of effect, and side‑effect profile, allowing clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to the individual patient’s physiological status, surgical requirements, and risk factors. Understanding the full spectrum of these agents enhances patient safety, optimizes peri‑operative management, and supports informed selection of the most appropriate anesthetic regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1324
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics encompass a diverse group of agents that are used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness for surgical and diagnostic procedures. The class of general anesthetics includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný (nitrous oxide), and propofol. Each of these agents has distinct pharmacologic characteristics, such as differences in onset speed, duration of action, potency, and side‑effect profile, which influence their selection for specific clinical scenarios. Understanding the full spectrum of general anesthetic agents—ranging from the historic inhalational agent chloroform to the widely used intravenous agent propofol—allows clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to individual patient needs, procedural requirements, and safety considerations, thereby optimizing peri‑operative care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2045
retrieved_docs.content: Anesthetics, general constitute a broad class of agents used to produce reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia for surgical procedures. This class includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný (nitrous oxide), and propofol, each of which is a specific child of the general anesthetic category. In addition, xenon is classified as a type of general anesthetic. Together, these diverse compounds—ranging from volatile liquids such as chloroform and isofluran to intravenous agents like propofol and ketamin, and to gaseous agents such as nitrous oxide and xenon—provide clinicians with multiple options for tailoring anesthetic care to the physiological needs, safety considerations, and procedural requirements of individual patients. Understanding the relationships among these agents supports informed selection, dosing, and monitoring strategies in peri‑operative medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2055
retrieved_docs.content: Halothane is contraindicated for use in patients receiving metaraminol, indicating that the inhalational anesthetic should be avoided when the vasopressor metaraminol is administered due to potential adverse interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Methoxyfluran, a halogenated ether anesthetic, is recognized for its ability to induce فقدان الوعي (loss of consciousness), which is a central therapeutic objective in the administration of general anesthesia. By depressing central nervous system activity, methoxyfluran reliably produces the unconscious state required for surgical procedures, highlighting its role as an effective agent for achieving controlled loss of consciousness in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314

In addition, xenon is identified as a specific type of general anesthetic. Together, these substances represent the diverse pharmacologic options used to achieve reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia during surgical and procedural care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2243
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a class of drugs used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness for surgical procedures, and they are collectively referred to in Arabic as “التخدير العام.” This category includes a diverse group of agents that are considered “children” of the general anesthetic class, such as chloroform, enflurane, fencyclidine, flurothyl, isoflurane, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. In addition, xenon is classified as a type of general anesthetic, illustrating that both traditional volatile compounds (e.g., chloroform, enflurane, isoflurane) and newer agents (e.g., propofol, methohexital, xenon) belong to the same therapeutic group. Understanding that these substances share the common purpose of producing general anesthesia helps clinicians select appropriate agents based on pharmacologic properties, safety profiles, and specific patient needs.

retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a broad class of agents employed to produce reversible loss of consciousness for surgical procedures, and this class includes a variety of specific compounds. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1786

This group includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný, and propofol. Each of these compounds possesses distinct pharmacologic characteristics but shares the common purpose of inducing and maintaining a reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical and diagnostic procedures. Recognizing that chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný, and propofol are all members of the general anesthetic class helps clinicians select appropriate agents, tailor anesthetic plans to individual patient needs, and optimize peri‑operative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2193
retrieved_docs.content: Conivaptan produces increased diuresis as a physiological effect, but it is contraindicated in patients with urinary retention (انقطاع البول). Bisoprolol also lists urinary retention (انقطاع البول) as a contraindicated condition. Consequently, both conivaptan and bisoprolol should be avoided in individuals who are unable to void urine, highlighting the importance of assessing bladder function before prescribing these medications.

Fenoprofen is contraindicated in hypersensitivity, bisoprolol in urinary retention, and tolnaftate in hypersensitivity. These interconnections delineate the chemical distinctions, therapeutic classifications, physiological actions and safety constraints essential for informed clinical decision‑making and patient care.
